5. Toy Models

5.1. Balanced bracket

Architecture

Claim: “A transformer has an inductive bias towards vectorized operations, because at each sequence position the same weights “execute”, just on different data. So if we want to “think like a transformer”, we want to get away from procedural for/if statements and think about what sorts of solutions can be represented in a small number of transformer weights.”

def is_balanced_vectorized(tokens: Float[Tensor, "seq_len"]) -> bool:
    """
    Return True if the parens are balanced.

    tokens is a vector which has start/pad/end indices (0/1/2) as well as left/right brackets (3/4)
    """
    # Convert start/end/padding tokens to zero, and left/right brackets to +1/-1
    table = t.tensor([0, 0, 0, 1, -1])
    change = table[tokens]
    # Get altitude by taking cumulative sum
    altitude = t.cumsum(change, -1)
    # Check that the total elevation is zero and that there are no negative altitudes
    no_total_elevation_failure = altitude[-1] == 0
    no_negative_failure = altitude.min() >= 0

    return (no_total_elevation_failure & no_negative_failure).item()

5.2. Othello

Neeel Nanda blog: OthelloGPT is given the move history and is tasked with predicting the next legal move. The result is that the model learns an emergent world representation as it computes the state of the board as an intermediate goal (they extract this via non-linear probes and try casual interventions, Neel Nanda says you can do linear)

We tried to mech interp from scratch.

Some hypotheses:

  • Input approximates bigram component

Observations:

  • First move
    • for legal moves the logit attribution is to the final MLP (L7):
    • For illegal moves the logit attribution is negative from columns
  • Second move

    • for legal moves the logit attribution is spread out between input and MLPs, in particular L5 and L6, but not L7

    • for illegal moves the logit attribution is spread between last row and last column

  • Thirtieth move
  • Fiftieth move

    • Legal move 8

    • Illegal move 53

Looking at what moves attention heads are looking at:

  • Layer 0 Head 0: Your old placements
  • Layer 0 Head 3: Their old placements
  • Layer 3 Head 7: Might look at colors
  • Layer 4 Head 5: Previous move only
  • Layer 7 head 7: Maybe tokens it’s gonna capture???

Attention heads don’t seem to be doing anything smart. Looking at activations with linear probes next. “Given this head/MLP output, can a linear probe tell whether occupied cell X belongs to me or them?”

Using only the layer 6 MLP output at the current move position, the trained linear probes correctly predicted mine vs theirs for about 88.6% of occupied board-cell examples in the test set.

We showed that a linear probe can predict ownership of cells even in the late game. Hence the model stores state.

1.5.2 Grokking and Modular arithmetic 1.5.3 OthelloGPT : Explore LLM trained on the Othello game 1.5.4 Superposition and SAEs: Famous anthropic paper “Toy Models of Superposition”. SAEs were originally developed to address this.
